---
summary: 'Every Centraid app is the same folder shape — index.html, app.json, queries/, actions/, migrations/. Walkthrough using the Hydrate template.'
title: 'App anatomy'
---

# App anatomy

A Centraid app is a folder with a fixed shape. The gateway recognizes it; nothing else needs to be configured.

What you author is the **source tree** below — static UI files, JavaScript handlers, and SQL migrations. There is **no build step**: handlers are plain `.js` ES modules that the runtime loads directly. Type-checking, if you want it, comes from JSDoc annotations resolved against the `@centraid/openclaw-plugin` types package — never from a compile step.

The source tree holds two halves of one idea: a **tool catalog** declared in `app.json` (the `queries[]` and `actions[]` arrays), and **implementations** of those tools under `queries/` and `actions/`. The static files (`index.html`, CSS, JS) are the UI that calls into the tool catalog. See [Queries and actions](/concepts/queries-and-actions) for the framing.

```
my-app/                       # source tree (what you author)
├── app.json                  # manifest (required)
├── package.json              # devDependency on @centraid/openclaw-plugin (editor types only)
├── index.html                # entry HTML (required if the app has a UI)
├── app.css                   # optional, served as static
├── app.js                    # optional, served as static
├── tokens.css                # frozen design-tokens snapshot (scaffolded)
├── queries/
│   └── <name>.js             # read-only handlers
├── actions/
│   └── <name>.js             # write handlers
├── migrations/
│   └── 0001_init.sql         # schema, numbered from 0001
└── automations/              # optional — scheduled background work
    └── <id>/
        ├── automation.json   # manifest (prompt, trigger, requires)
        └── handler.js        # the JS the scheduler fires
```

The runtime loads `.js` files only. Do not add a `tsconfig.json`, `*.ts` handlers, or any bundler — they have no effect at runtime and are rejected on upload. The `package.json` exists purely so an editor can resolve the `@centraid/openclaw-plugin` types for IntelliSense; run `bun install` (or `npm install`) once if you want that, and it changes nothing at runtime.

> A freshly scaffolded app does **not** ship empty `queries/`, `actions/`, or `migrations/` directories — the git-backed store tracks files, not empty folders. Those directories appear the moment the first handler or migration is written.

The app `id` is a plain slug: lowercase `a–z` / `0–9` / `-`, 1–63 chars, no leading `_`, **no dots**. It must match the app's folder name and URL slug.

## Static asset allowlist

The gateway serves any file whose extension matches the upload allowlist:

```
.html .htm .css .js .mjs .json .md .txt .svg .sql
.png .jpg .jpeg .webp .gif .ico .woff .woff2 .ttf .otf .map
```

Anything outside the allowlist — and any binary executable, native module, or symlink — is refused on upload.

These names are runtime-owned and must never be committed (they are rejected on upload):

- `data.sqlite` — the app's database, written by handlers only
- `runtime.sqlite` — chat sessions / run ledger / automation state, gateway-owned
- `current.json`, `_registry.json`, `versions/` — gateway-owned version artifacts

`app.json` is read by the dispatcher, not served as a static page. The `queries/` and `actions/` directories are invoked through the dispatcher, not served.

## Walkthrough: Hydrate

The Hydrate template is the simplest non-trivial app — one table, one query, one action, four user knobs, one UI screen. Use it as a reference shape. (`packages/blueprints/apps/hydrate/`.)

### `app.json` (excerpt)

```json
{
  "manifestVersion": 1,
  "id": "hydrate",
  "name": "Hydrate",
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "hydrate_daily",
      "columns": [
        { "name": "date", "type": "TEXT" },
        { "name": "cups", "type": "INTEGER" }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "actions": [
    {
      "name": "set-cups",
      "description": "Set today's cup count (clamped 0..8).",
      "confirmation": "none",
      "input": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": { "cups": { "type": "number", "minimum": 0, "maximum": 8 } },
        "required": ["cups"],
        "additionalProperties": false
      },
      "writes": ["hydrate_daily"]
    }
  ],
  "queries": [
    {
      "name": "get-today",
      "input": { "type": "object", "properties": {}, "additionalProperties": false },
      "reads": ["hydrate_daily"]
    }
  ]
}
```

### `migrations/0001_init.sql`

All schema lives in migrations. Hydrate's first migration creates every table the app uses — including the recap table a later feature reads — because v0 apps **fold all schema into `0001_init.sql`** until a version ships (see [Migrations](/build/migrations)).

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS hydrate_daily (
  date TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  cups INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0
);

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS hydrate_weekly_recaps (
  week_ending TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  total_cups INTEGER NOT NULL,
  goal_hits INTEGER NOT NULL,
  encouragement TEXT NOT NULL,
  generated_at INTEGER NOT NULL
);
```

### `queries/get-today.js`

A query handler is a single-argument async function. Destructure what you need — here just `db`. Every `db` call returns a Promise; always `await`.

```js
export default async ({ db }) => {
  const today = todayKey();
  const row = await db.prepare('SELECT cups FROM hydrate_daily WHERE date = ?').get(today);
  return { date: today, cups: row ? row.cups : 0, goal: 8 };
};
```

### `actions/set-cups.js`

An action handler receives the validated input as `body`. It may return its payload directly, or wrap it as `{ status, body }` (the legacy shape — the dispatcher unwraps `body` and treats `status >= 400` as an error).

```js
export default async ({ body, db }) => {
  const next = Math.max(0, Math.min(8, Number(body?.cups ?? 0)));
  await db
    .prepare(
      `INSERT INTO hydrate_daily (date, cups) VALUES (?, ?)
       ON CONFLICT(date) DO UPDATE SET cups = excluded.cups`,
    )
    .run(todayKey(), next);
  return { status: 200, body: { date: todayKey(), cups: next, goal: 8 } };
};
```

### `app.js`

The UI never talks to the dispatcher by hand. The runtime bakes a `window.centraid` bridge into every served HTML page — call `.read()` and `.write()`, and subscribe to writes with `.onChange()`:

```js
async function refresh() {
  const { cups } = await window.centraid.read({ query: 'get-today', input: {} });
  document.getElementById('count').firstChild.data = String(cups);
}

document.getElementById('add').addEventListener('click', async () => {
  const cur = Number(document.getElementById('count').firstChild.data);
  await window.centraid.write({ action: 'set-cups', input: { cups: cur + 1 } });
});

// Re-fetch whenever the server observes a write (chat-assistant edits,
// a second window, an automation). The bridge auto-reconnects on drops.
window.centraid.onChange(() => void refresh());
refresh();
```

See [UI and changes](/build/ui-and-changes) for the full bridge API and the change-stream contract.

## Knobs (user-facing settings)

`app.json` can declare a `knobs[]` array — each entry renders as a control in the desktop shell's per-app settings popover. The four standard knobs every scaffolded app ships are font, width, corner radius, and accent color:

```json
{
  "knobs": [
    {
      "key": "appFont",
      "label": "Font",
      "type": "segmented",
      "default": "sans",
      "options": [
        { "value": "sans", "label": "Sans" },
        { "value": "serif", "label": "Serif" }
      ]
    },
    {
      "key": "appColor",
      "label": "Color",
      "type": "swatch",
      "default": "#4950F6",
      "options": [
        { "value": "#4950F6", "label": "Blue" },
        { "value": "#7C5BD9", "label": "Violet" }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

Knob values arrive in the iframe via the `centraid:settings` postMessage event, and the runtime also bakes them into `<html data-app-*>` on the initial response — see [UI and changes](/build/ui-and-changes#receiving-settings).
